methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $28M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 23 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 53.2x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Radian Aerospace is a Seed-stage Space & Aerospace company that has raised $27.5M to date. The sector comprises 5 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 private peers including SpaceX, Rocket Lab, Blue Origin, Planet Labs, and Firefly Aerospace. However, the narrow peer set—no same-stage Seed comparables and only one adjacent-stage private company—limits direct benchmarking precision.

Of three valuation methods tested, only Stage-Progression proves applicable. Using total capital raised ($27.5M) as a proxy for current post-money valuation and applying a standard 2.5x step-up multiplier from Seed to subsequent stage yields $69M. Public-Comp Anchoring cannot be applied due to the absence of disclosed revenue for the target; the Space & Aerospace sector median trades at 53.2x P/S, but this multiple is unusable without Radian's revenue figure. Peer-Median analysis is inapplicable given insufficient comparable data points within tightly defined filters.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $58M–$79M, centered on $69M. This range reflects a conservative 15% discount and 15% premium around the Stage-Progression output to account for execution risk and upside optionality. Key uncertainties include the absence of revenue visibility, lack of same-stage peer benchmarks, and limited disclosure on product maturity or customer traction. A fuller valuation would require either disclosed financials or a more densely populated seed-stage aerospace comparables set.
